column1: # say-it community pronunciation dictionary — General American only.
#   2. ipa                primary GenAm IPA, with stress marks (for display + future SSML backends)
#   3. respelling_us      English-like phonetic respelling, fed verbatim to macOS `say` (the path that actually works today)
#   5. alt_respelling_us  `|`-separated alternative respellings, paired with alt_ipa by index, or empty
#   6. source_url         best citation: documented pronunciation page, official FAQ, Wikipedia anchor, etc. Leave empty if unverified.
#   7. source_label       human-readable source label (e.g. "GNU Project official", "Wikipedia § Pronunciation")
#   8. category           project | product | cli-tool | tool | cs-term | acronym | abbreviation
#                         Only use `creator-clarified` when source_url documents a creator quote / official FAQ entry.
# Why English respelling rather than IPA/PHON phonemes?
#   macOS `say` (2026 era) does not parse `[[inpt PHON]]` markup or SSML `<phoneme>` tags — both are read as literal
#   text. So we feed plain English-like spellings that the TTS's internal letter-to-sound rules render acceptably.
#   The `ipa` column is retained for display, for future cloud TTS, and for the Windows SAPI backend (which DOES
#   - All entries are best-effort and welcome PRs to improve. Confidence should reflect what the source URL actually
#     proves, not what you believe to be true. When in doubt, mark `community-consensus` and let a future PR upgrade

column10: K8s community readings vary widely. "koob-control" is heard from Kelsey Hightower and many maintainers; "cube-cuddle" is the running meme; some say it letter-by-letter.
engine-x is the documented reading from the official site.
Wilhite (creator): "It's pronounced JIF, not GIF." Community remains split.
Crockford uses "JAY-son"; "JEE-son" is also widespread. Wikipedia notes the variance.
GOOEY is mainstream (matches Mandarin 故意 sound); "G-U-I" letter-by-letter is RMS/old-school; "gwee" is rare.
One syllable, hard G, rhymes with "new". Page is a dedicated pronunciation FAQ.
kates (8 letters between K and s) — community shorthand.
gra-FAH-na is the most common community reading.
KAY-native — the K is voiced, like saying the letter K then "native". Maintainer usage in talks (CNCF / KubeCon sessions); no official FAQ entry on knative.dev as of this writing.
RED-iss. Wikipedia opens with 'Redis (/ˈrɛdɪs/)' citing the Redis FAQ; Salvatore Sanfilippo (antirez, creator) uses the same reading in screencasts.
Linus's own pronunciation is "LIN-ux" (short I, schwa). Wikipedia documents the variants.
DEB-ee-uhn — portmanteau of Debra + Ian Murdock, documented on the about page.
FAQ documents "JANG-go" — silent D, like jazz guitarist Django Reinhardt.
view. Vue 2 project README states verbatim: 'Vue (pronounced /vjuː/, like view)'. Same reading echoed on en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vue.js.
veet. Project README states verbatim: 'Vite (French word for "quick", pronounced /viːt/, like "veet")'. README also links an mp3 of the pronunciation.

pie-DAN-tic — "py" pronounced "pie" (as in Python), then "dantic". Heard from Samuel Colvin (creator) in Talk Python To Me / FastAPI talks; no official FAQ entry on docs.pydantic.dev as of this writing.
Lamport: "lay-tek" (or "lah-tek"); never "lay-teks". Documented on the about page.
Knuth: like "tech" — the X is a Greek chi.
char (rhymes with "car") in C/C++ camp; "care" and "kar" also used.
JOO-pi-tur — named after Jupiter (planet) + Julia, Python, R.

Standard/now-famous reading is "an-THROP-ik" (stress on second syllable THROP), identical to the adjective "anthropic"; matches the company's own usage. The "an-THROW-pick" reading is a common mistake.
One syllable, rhymes with "fraud". Anthropic's LLM family.
jacks, one syllable. Google's autograd library.
QUADRANT — common reading; project FAQ TBD as a citable source.
quick — yes, intentional. Heard from creator Misko Hevery.
shad-C-N — common community reading; creator clarification TBD as a citable source.
German for "state": "TSOO-shtund" in German; most English speakers say "ZOO-stund". Creator clarification TBD.
NEE-oh-vim is the most common community reading.
Rich Hickey has said it sounds like "closure". Community reading.
LOO-ah — Portuguese for "moon". Documented on the about page.

WOZ-um is most common; "WAHS-um" also heard.
G-R-P-C letter-by-letter. (Some say "gee-R-P-C" stressing initial g.)
OH-awth most common; "O-auth" letter-by-O-then-auth also heard.
jot (RFC pronunciation per Section 1) — but letter-by-letter "J-W-T" dominates in spoken practice.
cider — like the apple drink. Documented in CIDR specs.
gleem — one syllable, like the verb.
rock — one syllable, named after the mythical bird.
plad — one syllable, like the fabric.
mar-EE-mo — Japanese for the moss balls.
DAP-er — like the adjective dapper. Distributed Application Runtime.
YAY-ger — German pronunciation; some say "JAY-ger" too.

Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining; letters "K-D-D".
Conference on Learning Theory; "colt" like the horse.
International Joint Conference on AI; letters dominant, "IJ-kai" also heard.
Special Interest Group on Computer Graphics; "SIG-graf".
Bilingual Evaluation Understudy; pronounced "blue" per the original paper.
Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation; French "rouge" — "roozh".
MT eval metric; like the meteor in the sky.
Consensus-based Image Description Evaluation; "CIDER" like the drink.
Intersection over Union (== Jaccard index); letters "I-o-U".
Mean Average Precision; spoken letters "m-A-P" or as "map" — contested.
Area Under the Curve (usually under ROC); letters "A-U-C".
Fréchet Inception Distance; letters dominant, "fid" sometimes.
Evidence Lower BOund; "EL-bow" like the joint.
Leonid Vaserštejn — German-style "VAH-ser-shtine" is closest to source; "WAH-ser-stine" common in ML talks.
French mathematician Maurice Fréchet; "fray-SHAY" — silent t.
Vision Transformer; letters dominant, "vit" used informally.
A Lite BERT; pronounced like the name "al-BERT".
Embeddings from Language Models; "EL-mo" like the Sesame Street character.
Denoising seq2seq pre-train; "bart" like the name.
Sub-quadratic Transformer alternative; "hi-EE-nuh" like the animal.
(IA)³ — Infused Adapter by Inhibiting and Amplifying Inner Activations; letters + "three".
Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models; letters or "dee-dim".
Elucidating the Design Space of Diffusion Models; letters "E-D-M".
Classifier-Free Guidance (also Context-Free Grammar in NLP); letters "C-F-G".
χ² test — the chi is the Greek letter, pronounced "kye"; NOT "chee" or "chai".
t-distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding; letters or "tee-snee".
Andrew Barto (RL textbook co-author with Sutton); "BAR-toh".
John Hopfield (Hopfield networks; 2024 Nobel in Physics); "HOP-feeld".
Judea Pearl (causal inference, Bayesian networks); "joo-DEE-uh PURL".
Bernhard Schölkopf (kernel methods, causal ML); "SHURL-kopf" (ö ≈ "ur"); English speakers often say "SHELL-kopf".
Max Tegmark (MIT, AI safety, Future of Life Institute); "TEG-mark".
FYNE-toon — continue training a pretrained model on task data. Verb + compound.
pree-TRAIN — train from scratch on a large unlabeled corpus (the base step before fine-tuning).
uh-DAP-ter — small bottleneck layer injected into a frozen backbone (LoRA, IA³, etc. are adapter methods).
SFT on instruction-style (prompt, response) pairs; the step that turns a base LM into an instruct model.
Learn a soft-prompt embedding while the model stays frozen.
Tune a learned prefix prepended to each layer's keys/values.
in-STRUKT — short for instruction-tuned (GPT-3.5 Instruct, Llama-2-Instruct, etc.).
FEW-shot — solve a task from k examples in the prompt; no weight updates.
ZEE-roh-shot — solve a task with no in-context examples.
Reuse representations learned on one task for another; the parent concept of fine-tuning.

column3: kun tin yoo ul ler ning
column10: Sequential training across tasks without catastrophic forgetting; "kun-TIN-yoo-ul learning".

column3: kuh rik yu lum ler ning
column10: Train on easy examples first, then harder; "kuh-RIK-yu-lum learning".
Power-law relations between compute / data / params and loss; "SKAY-ling laws".
DOWN-STREEM — the application/eval task that a pretrained model is adapted to.
uh-LYNE-munt — making model behavior match human intent and values.
JAYL-brake — prompt or attack that bypasses an LM's safety policies.
RED-teeming — adversarial probing of an LM to find unsafe outputs.
RM in RLHF — scores responses to drive PPO/DPO; "rih-WARD model".
PREF-er-uns — pairwise (A > B) judgements collected for preference learning.
Helpful, Honest, Harmless — Anthropic's alignment goal triad; letters "H-H-H".
GROWN-ding — tying generations to verifiable evidence (retrieved docs, tool output).
huh-LOO-sih-NAY-shun — an LM's confident but ungrounded fabrications.
AY-junt — an LM-driven loop that perceives, plans, calls tools, and acts.
ay-JEN-tik — adjective; capable of multi-step autonomous action.
TOOL-YOOS — LM-invoked external functions (search, code exec, calc).
PROMPT — the input string fed to an LM.
REE-zun-ing — multi-step inference; also the name of the model class (o1, R1, etc.).

Initialism for Remote Direct Memory Access — read as four separate letters R-D-M-A (ar-dee-em-AY), not as a word. Backbone of multi-node GPU training (GPUDirect RDMA, RoCE, InfiniBand). Note the related RoCE is spoken "rocky", but RDMA itself stays letter-by-letter.

column7: allenai/molmo GitHub README ("Molmo: Multimodal Open Language Model")
column10: Ai2 (Allen Institute for AI) open multimodal model family, 2024; Molmo 2 in 2025-2026. Name is a portmanteau acronym: M(ultimodal) O(pen) L(anguage) Mo(del) = MOL + Mo. STRESS first syllable: MOL-moh, where MOL rhymes with "doll" (short o, same OL as in sibling OLMo) and "moh" is the long-o of "Model". Easy to confuse with sibling OLMo ("OL-mo") and to mispronounce as long-o MOHL-moh (like "mole") — the short-o MOL form follows the acronym. Respelling uses "mahl" because macOS say reads "mol"/"moll"/"mohl" all as long-o; only "mahl" yields the short-o. No official creator pronunciation FAQ found, so community-consensus.

Portmanteau of flash + inference; a high-performance GPU kernel/attention library for LLM serving (MLSys 2025, arXiv 2501.01005), a default backend in vLLM/SGLang/TensorRT-LLM. The infer element is from inference and is said like the verb infer (in-FUR), not in-fur as guessed from spelling. STRESS: primary on first syllable, FLASH-in-fur. No creator-quoted pronunciation found, so community-consensus.

AI-assisted coding: you prompt an LLM and accept generated code on 'vibes'. Coined by Andrej Karpathy Feb 2025; Collins Word of the Year 2025; in Merriam-Webster slang. STRESS on first word: VYBE-koh-ding. Key for non-natives: 'vibe' has a long i and rhymes with 'tribe' — NOT 'vib'.

Real-time voice-AI lab (Stanford 2023; Karan Goel, Albert Gu) behind the Sonic TTS models, built on state-space models (SSMs); rising ElevenLabs rival. Name derives from "Cartesian" (math). STRESS: kar-TEE-zhuh — soft "zh" as in "vision". Cartesia's own docs give IPA kʰ ɑ ɹ tʲ i ʒ ɐ, formed by taking "cartesian" and replacing the final "-an" with an "uh" sound.

k-bit quantization library for PyTorch underpinning 8-bit optimizers, LLM.int8() and 4-bit/NF4 QLoRA. Written as one lowercase token but spoken as three words: BITS-and-BYTES — STRESS on first and last, middle 'and' reduced to schwa. The spelling is the pronunciation; only the concatenation obscures the word boundaries.

Open-source multi-agent terminal coding agent by CodebuffAI; benchmarks against Claude Code. Name is "code" + "buff" (buffed-up / enhanced). STRESS first syllable: CODE-buff. "buff" rhymes with "stuff"/"rough" (NOT "byoof"). No creator-documented pronunciation found, but both halves are everyday English words so the reading is unambiguous. Formerly named Manicode.

column7: arXiv 2302.04761 — Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools (Schick et al., Meta AI)
column10: Meta AI paper (Schick et al., 2023): seminal "LLM teaches itself to use tools/APIs" work, foundational to tool-calling agents. Portmanteau of "tool" + "-former" (borrowed from Transformer). STRESS: TOOL-for-mer; "-former" rhymes with the end of transFORMER. No creator audio on record, but the construction is unambiguous.

Multi-head Latent Attention, the DeepSeek-V2/V3 attention variant that compresses the KV cache into a latent vector. Spoken as an initialism, letter by letter: em-el-AY (STRESS on the final letter). Not pronounced as a word. Collides with other meanings of MLA (Modern Language Association, etc.), so in ML context spell it out and rely on the surrounding words Multi-head Latent Attention.

column7: arXiv 2306.00978 — AWQ: Activation-aware Weight Quantization for LLM Compression and Acceleration (MIT HAN Lab)
column10: Activation-aware Weight Quantization. Spoken "awk" (like the unix tool) or spelled out A-W-Q.

Dominant Rust terminal-UI crate (powers gitui and many TUIs); community fork of tui-rs (2023). Official README documents the IPA ˌræ.təˈtu.i; named after the dish/Pixar film Ratatouille. STRESS: rat-uh-TOO-ee (primary stress on TOO).

column7: arXiv 2402.01030 — "Executable Code Actions Elicit Better LLM Agents" (Wang et al., ICML 2024)
column10: Coined compound of "Code" + "Act(ion)" from the ICML 2024 paper; the agent framework that lets LLMs emit executable Python as their action space (underpins OpenHands / CodeActAgent). Two plain English words; STRESS first syllable: CODE-act (noun-compound stress), not code-ACT.

column7: Claude Code Docs — Create custom subagents
column10: Compound of "sub-" + "agent"; a specialized AI assistant that runs in its own context window, used heavily in Claude Code, the Claude Agent SDK, and agentic-coding tooling. STRESS: SUB-ay-jent — primary on "sub", "agent" keeps its /ˈeɪdʒənt/ ("ay-jent"); the "g" is soft (j), not hard. Spelled as one word in the official docs.

column7: Wikipedia: Qwen (通义千问 / Tōngyì Qiānwèn)
column10: Alibaba's Tongyi (通义) brand umbrella over Qwen / Tongyi Qianwen (通义千问, pinyin Tōngyì Qiānwèn), surfaced across Alibaba Cloud Model Studio and Hugging Face. Pinyin Tōngyì: tōng (通, rounded vowel of English "song") + yì (义, "yee"). STRESS-pattern: TOHNG-yee, roughly even with a slight lift on the first syllable. English speakers often mangle it as "tongue-ee" or "tahn-jee"; the o is the rounded "ong" of "song," not "tongue."

Cross-platform Rust file-watcher that runs a command on file changes (alternative to entr/nodemon). Name = "watch" + "exec" (execute). The "exec" suffix follows dev-tooling convention (cf. the exec syscall / shell builtin): "eg-ZEK", not "ex-eck". STRESS: WOTCH-eg-zek (primary on first syllable, secondary on -ZEK). No official creator pronunciation FAQ exists, but the compound is unambiguous.

column7: QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs (arXiv 2305.14314)
column10: 4-bit NormalFloat, the information-theoretically-optimal 4-bit datatype introduced in the QLoRA paper (Dettmers et al., 2023) and shipped in bitsandbytes for quantized fine-tuning. Read as an initialism plus the digit: N-F-FOUR. "NF" = NormalFloat, "4" = 4-bit. Main stress on FOUR. Not "niff-four".

Reflexion is a foundational LLM-agent self-reflection technique (Shinn et al., 2023, "Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning", arXiv:2303.11366), cited across agent frameworks. Despite the 'x' spelling it is pronounced exactly like "reflection": rih-FLEK-shun. STRESS on second syllable (FLEK). The 'x' here is /kʃ/, not /ks/ + separate vowel — do not say "re-FLEX-ion"; non-natives sometimes second-guess against "reflection" but the two are homophones — "reflexion" is just the older British orthography. IPA per Oxford Learner's Dictionary /rɪˈflekʃn/.

column7: arXiv 2205.14135 — FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness (Dao et al.)
column10: IO-aware exact-attention GPU kernel (Dao-AILab; FlashAttention-2/3) baked into vLLM, TGI, TensorRT-LLM, PyTorch SDPA. Spoken as the two English words "flash" + "attention" run together, one word in writing. STRESS: secondary on FLASH, primary on -TEN- (flash-uh-TEN-shun). Non-native speakers hesitate on the flash+attention compound; pronunciation is just the plain English words.

column7: LG AI Research — EXAONE HuggingFace org page
column10: LG AI Research's open-weight LLM/LMM family (EXAONE 4.0/4.5, K-EXAONE; 2025-2026). Name is an acronym, EXpert AI for EveryONE, so it parses EX-A-ONE. STRESS: EX-a-wun (first syllable). NOT 'exa-ONE' like the SI prefix, NOT 'ex-AY-one.' Acronym documented on LG's HF org page; no explicit spoken-pronunciation quote, hence community-consensus.

column7: LMSYS Org Blog — "Fast and Expressive LLM Inference with RadixAttention and SGLang"
column10: SGLang's automatic KV-cache reuse mechanism — a radix tree over the KV cache for prefix sharing; coined by LMSYS (2024). Compound of "radix" + "attention". STRESS: RAY-diks-uh-TEN-shun (primary on RAY, secondary on TEN). "radix" is /ˈreɪdɪks/ (RAY-diks, the standard dictionary form per Wiktionary/Cambridge), NOT "ra-dicks" or "ra-tix"; final cluster is "-ks" not "-tx". Often mispronounced by non-native devs as "ratix"/"radicks". Alt /ˈrædɪks/ (RAD-iks) is a secondary variant.

Rust polyglot version manager + task runner by Jeff Dickey (jdx); the asdf successor. Name is short for the French culinary term "mise-en-place." Official docs: `mise` (pronounced "meez"), rhymes with "cheese." One syllable, voiced final z. Common wrong guesses: "my-z", "mice", "miz."

column7: Reka Core, Flash, and Edge technical report (arXiv 2404.12387)
column10: Reka AI — multimodal frontier-model lab (Reka Core / Flash / Edge). Reka publishes no official pronunciation, so usage is genuinely split. The name evokes the word for 'river' in two languages that pronounce it differently: South Slavic река gives REK-uh (short e), while Hungarian Réka gives REE-kuh (long ee); English speakers also default to REE-kuh. STRESS on first syllable: REK-uh or REE-kuh.

column5: ess dubble yoo ee ay jent
column7: arXiv 2405.15793 — SWE-agent: Agent-Computer Interfaces Enable Automated Software Engineering (Princeton/Stanford, NeurIPS 2024)
column10: Princeton/Stanford autonomous coding agent (Yang, Jimenez, Press et al.); "SWE" = software engineering. Pronunciation is genuinely split: most ML/coding-agent speakers say it as one syllable "SWEE" (rhymes with "free"), but spelling it out "S-W-E agent" is also common. No creator/docs/FAQ documents an official reading (SWE-bench FAQ and SWE-agent README both silent), so marked contested; the "swee" form is the more frequent spoken default and is used as the primary respelling. STRESS: SWEE-ay-jent.

column7: arXiv: GQA: Training Generalized Multi-Query Transformer Models from Multi-Head Checkpoints
column10: Grouped-Query Attention — the default attention in Llama 2/3, Qwen, Gemma, GPT-OSS. Read as an initialism, letter by letter: G-Q-A, jee-kyoo-AY, STRESS on the final letter. Said-as-a-word never caught on because the Q has no following vowel, so spelling it out is the dominant convention in papers, model cards, and talks.

Rust immediate-mode GUI library by Emil Ernerfeldt (@emilk). README states verbatim: "pronounced as 'e-gooey'. Please don't write it as 'EGUI'." Lowercase, like "e" + the word "gooey". STRESS: EE-gooey (first syllable). Often mispronounced "ee-gwee" or spelled out E-G-U-I.

ByteDance Seed's text-to-image model (Seedream 4.0/4.5, 2025-2026; 4K). Portmanteau of Seed + dream, parallel to siblings Seedance (Seed+dance, video) and SeedEdit — so SEED-reem, not see-DREEM. STRESS: SEED-reem (primary stress on first syllable). The Seed prefix is ByteDance's research division.

Open-source TypeScript AI agent framework from the ex-Gatsby team (YC-backed, 1.0 in early 2026). Coined name; etymology undocumented. Dominant English reading is MASS-truh — short A as in 'master', stress on the first syllable. A spelling-pronunciation MAH-struh is sometimes heard; no documented creator FAQ resolves it, hence community-consensus. STRESS: MASS-truh.

column7: arXiv 2108.12409 — Train Short, Test Long: Attention with Linear Biases (ALiBi), Press et al., ICLR 2022
column10: ALiBi = Attention with Linear Biases, a transformer position method used in BLOOM and MPT. The camelCase ALiBi cues that it reads as the everyday English word "alibi" (STRESS: AL-uh-by, first syllable) — not spelled out A-L-I-B-I, and the "-bi" is long-i ("by"), not "bee". Paper/GitHub don't document the pronunciation, so community-consensus, but the wordplay is unambiguous.

Cohere Labs' open-science multilingual LLM family (Aya 101 / Aya 23 / Aya Expanse / Aya Vision / Tiny Aya), up to 101 languages, Apache-2.0. Named after the Twi (Ghana) word 'aya' = 'fern', an Adinkra symbol of endurance and resourcefulness (per Cohere's announcements). Standard reading is AH-yah (/ˈɑːjə/), matching the Twi/Akan and common given-name pronunciation. STRESS first syllable: AH-yah. Some English speakers anglicize it to EYE-uh, but the fern/Twi origin points to AH-yah.

column7: PyTorch Tutorials — Getting Started with Fully Sharded Data Parallel (FSDP)
column10: Fully Sharded Data Parallel — PyTorch's ZeRO-style multi-GPU training strategy that shards parameters, gradients, and optimizer states across ranks. Initialism, spelled out letter-by-letter: EFF-ESS-DEE-PEE (no widely-used word pronunciation). Central to multi-GPU/LLM training docs since 2022; FSDP2 is the current rewrite.

DeepSeek's FP8/FP4 GEMM kernel library for Hopper/Blackwell, used as a MoE backend by vLLM/SGLang. "Deep" + "GEMM" (General Matrix Multiply). Pronunciation is genuinely split: most HPC/BLAS practitioners say GEMM as a single word rhyming with "gem"/"hem" (mirroring how S-GEMM = "ess-jem", D-GEMM = "dee-jem" are spoken), giving DEEP-jem. Others spell it out, deep-G-E-M-M. README gives no phonetic guidance. STRESS: DEEP-jem.

column7: Wiktionary — 海螺 (hǎiluó), Mandarin IPA /xaɪ̯ lu̯ɔ/
column10: Hailuo AI is MiniMax's text/image-to-video generator (Hailuo 2.3, Media Agent; 2025). Name romanizes Chinese 海螺 hǎiluó ("conch / sea snail"), hence the English nickname "Conch Video." STRESS: HIGH-lwaw. Two syllables: "hai"="high," "luo"=pinyin [lwɔ] — a single glide-syllable like "law" with a w-onset, NOT three beats "loo-oh." Common anglicized form is "HIGH-low" (/haɪloʊ/), shown as the alt. Mandarin tones hǎi (3rd) + luó (2nd); English speakers can ignore tone.

column7: Frantar et al., "GPTQ: Accurate Post-Training Quantization for Generative Pre-trained Transformers" (arXiv:2210.17323)
column10: Post-training weight quantization method for LLMs ("GPT" + "Q" for quantization). An initialism, spelled out letter-by-letter: jee-pee-tee-KYOO, primary stress on the final Q. The "GPT" prefix is read like GPT-4 (not "gee-putt"). Not pronounced as a single word.

column7: Hedra — Company / About page (a16z-backed AI video startup behind Character-3)
column10: Hedra AI — a16z-backed digital-human / talking-avatar startup (Character-3 omnimodal model; $32M Series A, May 2025; 10M+ videos generated). Name = Greek 'hedra' (ἕδρα, 'seat/base'), which standalone reads HED-ruh — Behind the Name lists [ˈhɛdrə]. STRESS: HED-ruh. No founder pronunciation on record; the HEE-druh variant (/ˈhiːdrə/) circulates by analogy with compounds like polyHEEdron / dodecaHEEdron, but for the bare name HED-ruh is the dictionary-backed reading. CONTESTED — no official guidance.

ByteDance's AI video-generation model family (Seedance 1.0 / 2.0, 2026); the video-side sibling of the Seedream image model, both under ByteDance's "Seed" foundation-model brand. Portmanteau of Seed + dance. STRESS: SEED-dance — say "seed" then "dance", NOT "see-dance". No documented creator pronunciation quote, but the Seed-prefix branding makes the split unambiguous across docs.

column7: arXiv 2407.01489 — "Agentless: Demystifying LLM-based Software Engineering Agents"
column10: Coined in the OpenAutoCoder/SWE-bench paper for an approach that solves coding tasks WITHOUT an autonomous agent loop — the deliberate antonym of "agentic". STRESS stays on the first syllable: AY-jent-less, not uh-JENT-less.

NVIDIA Collective Communications Library — GPU collective comms (all-reduce, all-gather) for distributed ML training. Official docs: "NCCL, pronounced 'Nickel'". Said like the coin/metal: NIK-uhl, stress on first syllable. Non-obvious from the letters N-C-C-L.

AI dev platform / VS Code + Cursor extension and company (YC W24) behind GPT Pilot. Name = the Greek mathematician Pythagoras (/pɪˈθæɡərəs/) minus the final -s. STRESS on second syllable: pih-THAG-uh-ruh. First syllable is short 'pih' in the US reading; the long-'pie' first syllable (alt) mirrors the standard British reading of Pythagoras. No creator-documented pronunciation found — inferred from the established English reading of Pythagoras.

DeepSpeed's ZeRO = "ZEro Redundancy Optimizer" (Microsoft, arXiv:1910.02054). Stylized capitalization (capital Z and O) signals it reads as the English word "zero" — identical to the number, NOT spelled out Z-E-R-O. STRESS: ZEER-oh. Stages are ZeRO-1/2/3 ("zero one/two/three"). Distinct from existing "zero-shot".

Multi-Query Attention — the single-KV-head attention variant that GQA generalizes; referenced in serving docs like TensorRT-LLM. Initialism read letter-by-letter, not as a word: STRESS final letter, em-kyoo-AY, exactly parallel to GQA. The expansion 'multi-query attention' is never pronounced.

Rust-based cross-platform conda/PyPI package & environment manager from prefix.dev (rising 2025-2026 Python/data-science workflow tool). Reads as the English word "pixie" — STRESS first syllable: PIK-see, rhymes with "fixie." Not "pix-eye." No official creator pronunciation quote located; based on the unambiguous English reading of the spelling. CLI invoked lowercase as `pixi`.

CLI dotfiles manager. Name = French "chez moi" ("at my house"). Official FAQ documents the pronunciation as /ʃeɪ mwa/ "shay-mwa". Non-obvious because the spelling looks English/German; the "z" is silent and "oi" is the French [wa], not English "oy".

Mixed-bitrate quantization format native to the ExLlamaV2 (turboderp) local-LLM inference library; the name is a contraction of ExLlamaV2 (EX-L + 2). Spelled out letter-by-letter "E-X-L-TWO" in local-LLM communities, not read as "excel-two". STRESS on TWO. File extension/suffix seen as .exl2 on Hugging Face quants.

Mistral's 22B code-generation model (released May 2024) with a fill-in-the-middle endpoint, widely used in IDE autocomplete (Continue.dev, Tabnine). The name is reported to be inspired by "orchestral" (the model harmonizing coding tasks), blended with "code" + the company name "Mistral." STRESS: koh-DESS-truhl — primary stress on the second syllable, by analogy with orchestral (or-KESS-truhl). A code-forward first-syllable reading KOH-duh-struhl (keeping the embedded "code" audible, echoing MISS-truhl) is also heard. Official docs give no phonetic guidance.

Microsoft Research functional language with algebraic effect handlers (Daan Leijen). Name from Japanese 効果 (kōka) "effect/effectiveness" — matching its effect-typing focus. STRESS first syllable: KOH-kuh, long-o then schwa. Japanese-faithful variant is KOH-kah (koh kah).

AI coding tool by AbanteAI — currently MentatBot, a GitHub-native coding agent (earlier archived CLI shared the name). Name borrowed from the "Mentats" (human computers) of Frank Herbert's Dune, from Latin mentis / mentation. STRESS-pattern: MEN-tat (first syllable). "tat" rhymes with "cat"; "men" rhymes with "ten".

NVIDIA's GPU-to-GPU interconnect (constant in GB200/H100 cluster talk). The "NV" prefix is the NVIDIA initialism, read as the letters N-V ("en-vee"), then plain "link": en-vee-LINK. Same NV-prefix pattern as NVSwitch. NOT "nuv-link" or "en-vid-ee-uh link" — a common non-native slip. STRESS: en-vee-LINK. No official FAQ documents the pronunciation; letter-initialism reading is universal in the community.

NVIDIA's CUDA C++ GEMM/linear-algebra template library; kernel backend behind vLLM, FlashInfer, and many 2025 LLM-serving stacks. The name is an acronym (CUDA Templates for Linear Algebra Subroutines and Solvers) deliberately landing on the sword 'cutlass' — say it exactly like that word. STRESS first syllable: KUT-luhs. NOT 'CUDA-lass' or 'cut-LASS'. Dictionary IPA /ˈkʌtləs/ (Oxford/Cambridge/Collins, same in BrE and AmE).

column7: Zhang & Sennrich, "Root Mean Square Layer Normalization", arXiv:1910.07467
column10: RMSNorm = Root Mean Square (Layer) Normalization, the normalization used in Llama/Mistral and most modern LLMs. Spoken as a compound: the "RMS" part is spelled out letter-by-letter (ar-em-ess), then "norm" said as the word. STRESS on NORM: "ar-em-ess NORM". No source documents an explicit creator pronunciation, but the letter-spelled-acronym + "norm" reading is universal in talks and writeups, hence community-consensus.

column7: arXiv 2309.06180 — Efficient Memory Management for Large Language Model Serving with PagedAttention
column10: vLLM's core attention algorithm, named after OS virtual-memory PAGING. Two parts: "paged" + "attention". STRESS: PAYJD uh-TEN-shun. The hazard is "paged" — it is /peɪdʒd/ (rhymes with "caged", one syllable, voiced -jd cluster), NOT two-syllable "page-ed". From the verb "to page" the KV cache into fixed-size blocks. Common in inference-infra discussion 2023-2026.

STRESS first syllable: MAT-uh-rin (short 'a' as in 'mat', NOT ma-TUR-in). PyO3's official CLI to build & publish Rust-based Python wheels (used by polars, pydantic-core, etc.; pip-installable). The README doesn't document a pronunciation, but 'Maturin' is a real French Huguenot given name (cf. Stephen Maturin in O'Brian's Aubrey–Maturin novels), and references consistently put the stress on the FIRST syllable. A yod variant 'MAT-yuh-rin' also occurs.

AI creative suite (image/video/3D generation) and the FLUX.1 Krea open model co-developed with Black Forest Labs (2025). Two-syllable name: KREE-uh — first syllable rhymes with "free," second is a schwa. STRESS on first syllable: KREE-uh. Common ESL trap: KRAY-uh; accepted reading is KREE-uh.

Google DeepMind's text-to-video model (Veo 3 / 3.1, 2025), used inside Gemini and Flow. Dominant English reading is VEE-oh; HowToPronounce gives /ˈviːoʊ/. A VAY-oh variant exists, echoing Spanish "veo" (I see). No official creator pronunciation is documented, hence contested. STRESS: VEE-oh.
OX-lint — the oxc-based linter; ox like the animal, not the letters O-X.

Mistral AI's multimodal model family (Pixtral 12B, Pixtral Large). Blend of 'pixel' + 'Mistral,' so '-tral' rhymes with sibling Mistral/Mixtral entries (TRAHL, long ah). Front-loaded stress PIKS-trahl is most common; second-syllable piks-TRAHL (matching some Mixtral speakers) also heard. No creator pronunciation guide published.

Zhipu AI / Tsinghua KEG open bilingual model series. Name = "Chat" + GLM, where GLM is an initialism for "General Language Model" spoken letter-by-letter: chat-G-L-M, NOT read as a word "glim". STRESS: chat-jee-ell-EM (final letter takes the beat). Bare "GLM" is already in the dict; this is the searched product name.

NVIDIA's flagship open LLM family (Nemotron 3: Nano/Super/Ultra, 2025-2026), hybrid Mamba-Transformer MoE for agentic reasoning. Coined name = NVIDIA's NeMo framework ("NEural MOdules", said NEE-mo) + the -tron suffix, so the prevailing reading is first-syllable-stressed NEE-moh-tron. Some devs mis-read it as nem-oh-TRON / nuh-MOT-ron; the NeMo etymology favors NEE-moh-tron. No official creator-documented pronunciation in text. STRESS: NEE-moh-tron.

Recraft V3 (Oct 2024), a 20B text-to-image model that topped the Artificial Analysis leaderboard for months. Name is the transparent compound re- + craft. STRESS on the root: re-CRAFT (ree-KRAFT), same pattern as the verb 'to recraft' — not RE-craft. No official creator pronunciation documented; natural English reading of the re- prefix compound.

JS/TS unused files/exports/deps detector by Lars Kappert (webpro). README states: "/ˈknɪp/ means '(to) cut' and is pronounced with a hard K" (Dutch). The K is NOT silent — say it "k-nip", NOT English "nip". Single short-i syllable as in "nip". macOS `say` silences the K on raw "knip", so the respelling uses a light schwa ("kuh nip") to force a hard-K onset — closest `say` gets to a true /kn/ cluster.
UL-truh-code — Claude Code max-effort mode (/effort) that fans work out across many subagents. Plain ultra + code.

Hidden-layer optimizer (MomentUm Orthogonalized by Newton-schulz) by Keller Jordan, used to set NanoGPT speedrun records and in large-scale pretraining. Pronounced like the subatomic particle: MYOO-on (two syllables), not "moo-on" or spelled out M-U-on.

DeepSeek's unified multimodal model (Janus / Janus-Pro) that decouples visual encoding for understanding vs generation. Named after the two-faced Roman god of doorways: JAY-nuhs, stress on the first syllable.
Genmo's open-source text-to-video model (Mochi 1). Pronounced like the Japanese rice cake: MOH-chee, not "MOTCH-ee" or "moh-CHAI".

Late-interaction visual document retrieval: ColBERT-style multi-vector matching over PaliGemma image patches. Said kol-PAH-lee — "Col" from ColBERT, "Pali" from PaliGemma (PAH-lee, the Buddhist canonical language).
MIT Han Lab's 4-bit inference engine that runs SVDQuant-compressed diffusion models. Named after the martial-arts weapon (nunchucks): NUN-chah-koo.

4-bit post-training quantization for diffusion models that shunts weight+activation outliers into a low-rank branch. SVD = Singular Value Decomposition, read letter-by-letter then "quant": ess-vee-dee-KWANT.
2024 modernized BERT-style encoder (8k context, RoPE, FlashAttention, GeGLU). Spoken MOD-ern-BERT — "BERT" as the single word "bert", never spelled out B-E-R-T.
DeepMind's foundation world model (Genie 1/2/3) that turns a single image or prompt into a playable, interactive environment. Pronounced like the lamp genie: JEE-nee.

Speculative-decoding method that adds multiple extra decoding "heads" so a model drafts several future tokens itself (no separate draft model). Named after the Gorgon Medusa with many snake-heads: muh-DOO-suh.
Black Forest Labs' in-context image generation+editing model (FLUX.1 Kontext). "Kontext" is German for "context"; said KON-text, stress on the first syllable.

Rust's unit of compilation / the package you publish to crates.io. Pronounced exactly like the wooden box "crate": KRAYT, one syllable, rhymes with "great" and "plate". Not "krah-tay" or "crayt-ee". (Asked by a non-native speaker in Discussion #34.)

The Rust compiler. The "c" is for "compiler" and is almost always read as the letter C: RUST-see (rust + C). A minority say "rust-cee" identically; "rustk" (one syllable) is heard but uncommon. Compare gcc/clang usage where the trailing letter is spoken.
Rust's official linter (run via `cargo clippy`). KLIP-ee — named after Clippy, the Microsoft Office paperclip assistant; same pronunciation as that mascot.
The Rust toolchain installer and version manager. Read as the two transparent parts: RUST-up (rust + up), stress on the first syllable. Not "rus-TUP".

column1: # --- CLI / agent-command vocabulary (Claude Code, Codex, git) ---

column10: Claude Code /simplify and everyday code review verb; stress the FIRST syllable — "SIM-pluh-fye".
The verb (Claude Code /compact — compact the conversation) stresses the SECOND syllable: "kum-PAKT". The adjective/noun ("a compact format") stresses the first: "KAHM-pakt".
The verb (claude --resume, resume a session): "rih-ZOOM". Don't confuse with the noun résumé "REZ-uh-may" — different word.
Clipped "initialize" (git init, npm init, /init). Most say "ih-NIT"; "IN-it" also common; some spell it out I-N-I-T.
DEP-ruh-kayt — three syllables, no "-shee-". Often mixed up with "depreciate" (deh-PREE-shee-ayt), which is a different word.
Code review, /review: "rih-VYOO", stress the second syllable.
git commit: "kuh-MIT", stress the second syllable.

column1: # --- frontier model names + world-model / diffusion / SDE paper jargon (added 2026-06-15) ---

column10: Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 model; ordinary English word 'FAY-bul', like the Opus/Sonnet/Haiku naming line.
Google's video diffusion model; French 'lumière' (light), 'loom-YAIR', stress on second syllable, not 'LOO-meer'.
Microsoft's multimodal model; Greek 'kosmos' (world/order), said like English 'cosmos' = 'KOZ-mos'.
Log-odds 'logistic unit'; long-o 'LOH-jit' is most common but short-o 'LAH-jit' is genuinely used too.
Aleatoric (data) uncertainty; from Latin 'alea' (dice), stress on 'TOR': both 'AY-' and 'AL-' first syllables are heard.
Epistemic (model) uncertainty; from Greek 'episteme' (knowledge), stress on third syllable as 'STEE' or 'STEM'.
Sigmoid Linear Unit activation (a.k.a. Swish); usually said as a word 'SEE-loo', sometimes spelled out 'S-I-L-U'.
French physicist Paul Langevin (Langevin dynamics); French 'lahnzh-VAN', not 'LANG-uh-vin'.
Japanese mathematician Kiyosi Itô (伊藤, Itô calculus); 'EE-toh', not 'EYE-toh'.
Mathematician Norbert Wiener (Wiener process); English 'WEE-ner' is common, German-faithful 'VEE-ner' also used.
After Dutch physicist Adriaan Fokker and German physicist Max Planck; 'FOK-er PLAHNGK', Planck like 'plonk'.
Austrian physicist Erwin Schrödinger (Schrödinger bridge); German 'SHROH-ding-er', not 'SHRAY-ding-er'.
After Dutch physicists Leonard Ornstein and George Uhlenbeck; 'ORN-stine OO-len-bek', Uhlenbeck not 'YOO-len-bek'.
Austrian mathematician Johann Radon (Radon-Nikodym derivative); English 'RAY-don', German-faithful 'RAH-don' also heard.
Aleksandr Lyapunov (stability theory); Russian, genuinely split between penultimate 'lee-AH-puh-nof' and original final-stress 'lyah-poo-NOF'.
Emmy Noether (Noether's theorem); German ö → 'NUR-ter', final th is a hard t, not English 'th'.
Bernard Koopman (Koopman operator); Dutch surname, long 'oh' → 'KOHP-man', not 'KOOP'.
After W. R. Hamilton (Hamiltonian dynamics); 'ham-il-TOH-nee-un', stress on third syllable.
Hermann von Helmholtz (free energy, Helmholtz machine); German 'HELM-holts', tz → 'ts'.
Max Planck (Planck constant); German 'PLAHNGK', rhymes roughly with English 'plank'.
After German statistician Emil Julius Gumbel (Gumbel-softmax); English 'GUM-bul' (rhymes with humble), German 'GOOM-bel'.
After German mathematician Ferdinand Georg Frobenius (Frobenius norm); German 'froh-BAY-nee-us', anglicized 'froh-BEE-nee-us'.
After American mathematicians Solomon Kullback and Richard Leibler (KL divergence); 'KOOL-bak LYE-bler', Leibler 'ei'='eye'.
After Soviet-Israeli mathematician Lev M. Bregman (Bregman divergence); Russian 'э'='eh', so 'BREG-man' not 'BRAYG-man'.
After American mathematician Richard Sinkhorn (Sinkhorn iteration); straightforward 'SINK-horn'.
After Greek-Brazilian physicist Constantino Tsallis (Tsallis entropy); anglicized 'SAL-iss', Greek-faithful 'TSAH-liss'.
After Hungarian mathematician Alfréd Rényi (Rényi entropy); Hungarian é='ay', ny=palatal n, so 'RAY-nyee'.
Adjective from German mathematician Bernhard Riemann (/ˈriːmɑːn/); 'ree-MAH-nee-un', not 'rye-'.
Bengali surname of statistician P. C. Mahalanobis; stress on 'NOH', 'mah-huh-luh-NOH-bis'.
German mathematician Rudolf Lipschitz; German 'sch' gives 'LIP-shits', often Anglicized to 'LIP-skits'.
German mathematician David Hilbert; 'HIL-bert', stress on first syllable.
French mathematician Henri Lebesgue; silent '-gue' gives 'luh-BEG', not 'luh-BESG'.
German mathematician Hermann Minkowski; German 'ming-KAWF-skee' vs common English 'ming-KOW-skee'.
Russian Pafnuty Chebyshev; closest is 'CHEB-ih-shoff' (-off ending), but English math often says 'cheb-ih-SHEV'.
French Jacques Hadamard, [adamaʁ] (silent final d); English speakers split between 'had-uh-MAR' and anglicized 'HAD-uh-mard'.
German Leopold Kronecker, [ˈkʁoːnɛkɐ]; 'KROH-neck-er' with stress on first syllable, not 'kro-NECK-er'.
German Otto Toeplitz, [ˈtœplɪts] (oe = ö); English usually anglicized 'TOPE-lits', also heard 'TEP-lits'.

column7: Official Qwen team blog (Alibaba Cloud), QwQ-32B-Preview release post
column10: Alibaba's Qwen-team reasoning model (QwQ = 'Qwen with Questions'). The official Qwen blog states verbatim: 'Note: This is the pronunciation of QwQ: /kwjuː/, similar to the word "quill".' So it's a single syllable: a 'kw' onset glued onto the sound of the letter Q (/juː/), i.e. KWYOO — NOT spelled out 'Q-W-Q' and NOT the kaomoji 'qwq' face. The creators' 'similar to quill' is an informal onset hint (shared 'kw-'), not a literal rhyme — /kwjuː/ does not end in an L and does not match quill's short 'i'. Lowercase middle 'w' makes it look like an emoticon, which is exactly why almost nobody guesses the intended sound.

column7: Tülu 3 paper (Allen Institute for AI), naming footnote
column10: AllenAI's open post-training model family (Tülu 2, Tülu 3). The Tülu 3 paper footnotes the name: 'A tülu is a hybrid camel bred between Bactrian camel and dromedary' — fitting AllenAI's running camel theme (the predecessor papers 'How Far Can Camels Go?' and 'Camels in a Changing Climate'). It comes from the Turkish word tülü ('hairy/feathered'); the ü is a front rounded vowel (like German ü or French 'tu'). English speakers overwhelmingly settle on TOO-loo with stress on the first syllable. A more faithful-to-Turkish rendering is tew-LOO — note Turkish stresses the FINAL syllable (Wiktionary: /tyˈly/). Common stumble: devs drop the umlaut entirely and say 'tuh-LOO' or 'TUH-luh', or read it as 'tulu' rhyming with 'Zulu'. (The AllenAI model name is spelled 'Tülu' with one ü; the underlying Turkish word is 'tülü'.)

column7: GaLore paper, 'Memory-Efficient LLM Training by Gradient Low-Rank Projection' (arXiv:2403.03507)
column10: Memory-efficient LLM training method = 'GRAdient LOw-Rank projEction' (ICML 2024 oral, jiaweizzhao/GaLore), pitched as a more memory-efficient cousin of LoRA. The casing 'GaLore' reads as a homograph of the English word 'galore' (/ɡəˈlɔːr/, stress on the SECOND syllable, as in 'savings galore') — which strongly suggests the intended reading is guh-LOR, a wink at LoRA. Note: this pun is an inference; the paper and authors don't state the pronunciation. Because the capital L splits it visually into Ga+Lore, many practitioners instead read it as an initialism with FIRST-syllable stress, GAL-or. Genuinely split in practice.

column7: CoCa paper, 'Contrastive Captioners are Image-Text Foundation Models' (Google Research, arXiv:2205.01917)
column10: Google Research's image-text foundation model; the name packs 'COntrastive CAptioner'. The CoCa casing reads naturally as KOH-kuh — a homograph of 'coca' (Coca-Cola / coca leaf) — which is the most common way it's said. Because it's an acronym, some letter it out C-O-C-A (see-oh-see-AY). Stress on the first syllable: KOH-kuh. No author statement fixes the pronunciation, so usage is unsettled. The hazard: the obvious reading collides with a loaded everyday word, making speakers second-guess and switch to spelling it out.

Apple's configuration-as-code language, open-sourced Feb 2024. The maintainers settle it explicitly in the launch post: 'We are delighted to announce the open source first release of Pkl (pronounced Pickle)'. So it is PICK-uhl, exactly like the cucumber. Stress on the first (only real) syllable: PICK-uhl. Because the name is spelled as three bare consonants with no vowels, almost everyone's first instinct is to spell it out 'P-K-L' or guess 'puh-KUL' / 'pee-kay-el' — all wrong.

Configuration/validation language (cuelang) by ex-Googler Marcel van Lohuizen, of Borg/GCL lineage. The official language spec opens with the answer verbatim: 'CUE, pronounced cue or Q, is a general-purpose and strongly typed constraint-based language.' So it is ONE syllable, kyoo, a homophone of the letter Q and of 'queue.' The all-caps stylization 'CUE' is widely glossed in the community as the backronym 'Configure Unify Execute' (the spec itself does not define the acronym), and that styling tricks people into spelling it 'C-U-E' as initials, which the project rejects.

column7: Introducing Kuma, the Service Mesh From Kong (Cloud Native Now)
column10: Envoy-based service mesh originally created and donated by Kong, accepted into the CNCF (now a Sandbox project; repo kumahq/kuma, branded with the bear emoji 🐻). The name is the Japanese word for 'bear' (くま / 熊), so the intended pronunciation follows Japanese: KOO-mah — stress on the FIRST syllable, 'oo' as in 'boot'. English speakers who don't know the origin reach for KYOO-mah (rhyming with 'puma'), which is the common mispronunciation. Standard Japanese pronunciation of くま is KOO-mah.

CNCF multi-cluster Kubernetes orchestrator (incubation project). The README opens with the etymology spelled out: 'Karmada (Kubernetes Armada)…' — a blend of Kubernetes + Armada. So the back half rhymes with 'armada': kar-MAH-duh, stress on the second syllable MAH, with a broad 'ah'. The trap is reading the first four letters as 'karm' (like karma) and saying KAR-ma-da with front stress; a secondary anglicization is kar-MAY-duh (mirroring the older 'ar-MAY-duh' variant of armada). Following the Spanish-rooted 'armada', kar-MAH-duh is the natural fit.

Grafana's eBPF auto-instrumentation tool (open-sourced 2023, github.com/grafana/beyla), since donated to OpenTelemetry as OpenTelemetry eBPF Instrumentation (OBI). Grafana Labs names projects after Norse mythology; Beyla is a figure from the Poetic Edda (Lokasenna), a servant of the god Freyr. Say it BAY-luh, stress on the FIRST syllable (first syllable rhymes with 'bay'/'they'). The 'ey' follows the standard English Anglicization of Old Norse names — the Bellows Poetic Edda pronouncing index lists 'Beyl-a' with 'ey as in they.' (Strict Old Norse is closer to [ˈbœylɑ], a front-rounded vowel, but English usage settles on the 'they' vowel.) English readers commonly mis-guess BYE-la (eye sound) or BEE-la, both wrong.

column7: Declarative Management of Kubernetes Objects Using Kustomize (kubernetes.io)
column10: Kubernetes-native config management tool (kubernetes-sigs/kustomize), built into kubectl since 1.14 as 'kubectl kustomize' and 'kubectl apply -k'. It is just the word 'customize' with the Kubernetes-style K-for-C swap, so it is said identically to customize: KUSS-tuh-myze, stress firmly on the first syllable. The hazard is purely the spelling — people see the leading 'K' and try to honor it as a hard 'kuh-STOM-ize', or spell-pronounce it letter-by-letter, especially since the sibling term 'Kubernetes' genuinely is hard. The joke is that the K is cosmetic (K8s, kubectl, Kustomize) and the word is said exactly like 'customize'.

The KDL Document Language (a config/serialization format, kdl.dev). The official FAQ answers the pronunciation question with simply "Same as 'cuddle'." Stress on CUH. Almost everyone defaults to spelling it out 'K-D-L' on first encounter; the maintainers deliberately want it said like the word cuddle (one implementation is even named 'cuddle'). Created by the kdl-org community (Kat Marchán et al.).

Google's data-templating / configuration language (common in Kubernetes / infra tooling). The official site states verbatim: "The name Jsonnet is a portmanteau of JSON and sonnet, pronounced 'jay sonnet'." So it's JAY (from JSON's 'jay-son') overlapping into SONNET — three syllables JAY-SON-it, primary stress on JAY (like JSON itself). Note the General American 'sonnet' vowel is /ɑː/, not British /ɒ/. Common mistakes: 'jay-SO-net', 'juh-SON-et', or reading it like the French name 'Jeannette'. The intended reading bakes in JSON (jay-son) + sonnet.

A programmable, non-Turing-complete configuration language by Gabriel Gonzalez (an alternative to YAML/JSON). The official README settles it: the name comes from a Dustman in the game Planescape: Torment (NOT author Roald DAHL and NOT the lentil dish 'dal/daal'), and 'rhymes with tall/call/hall — dɔl for a US speaker, dɔːl for a UK speaker.' So General American is DAWL (rhymes with TALL), stress on the single syllable. Despite the ruling, a large slice of the community still says /dɑːl/ ('dahl', like the dish), tracked in GitHub issue #144 which also catalogs /dæl/ (rhymes with 'pal'); the silent-h 'Dh' digraph even tempts a /ð/ ('that') onset.

Stress on the SECOND syllable: shuh-LESS-kee. Named after French mathematician/military officer André-Louis Cholesky (decomposition posthumously published 1924). Wikipedia gives /ʃəˈlɛski/ — the onset is 'sh', NOT a hard 'k'. The decomposition is everywhere in numerical computing / ML (covariance factorization, linear solvers, Gaussian processes), so the mispronunciation gets repeated constantly. English speakers overwhelmingly default to the spelling-driven 'koh-LESS-kee' (hard C), and many keep that even after being corrected, which is why it's genuinely contested rather than a clean single answer.

The 'oi' is French /wa/, so the first syllable is PWAH, not POY: PWAH-sawn. Named after French mathematician Siméon Denis Poisson. Wiktionary gives General American /ˈpwɑ.sɒn/ (no vowel-length mark) and RP /ˈpwɑː.sɒn/ ~ /ˈpwɑː.sɒ̃/ (the ː length and nasalized ending are RP, not GA). The dominant developer/student error is the fully anglicized POY-sun rhyming with 'poison', which is why the term reliably trips people in stats, queueing theory, and event-rate modeling. Educated stats usage converges on PWAH-sawn, so consensus rather than truly contested.

Two syllables. The 'ch' is French /ʃ/ (the 'sh' sound) and 'au' = /oʊ/, so it ends '-SHEE', never a hard 'ch' like 'church'. General American puts the stress on the SECOND syllable: koh-SHEE /koʊˈʃiː/ (per Wikipedia's US transcription and Dictionary.com). British English shifts stress to the FIRST syllable: KOH-shee /ˈkoʊʃi/ (the alt form) — so the stress is genuinely contested across dialects even though the consonants are agreed. Named after French mathematician Augustin-Louis Cauchy; English speakers meet it via the Cauchy distribution, Cauchy sequence, and Cauchy-Schwarz inequality. Routinely mangled as 'COW-chee' or 'CAW-chee' with a hard ch — both wrong.

Three syllables: BEZ-ee-ay, with the final '-ier' as the French /je/ → 'yay/ay' and a SILENT 'r'. Named after French engineer Pierre Bézier (French [bezje]); anglicized to 'BEZ-ee-ay' (Wikipedia also lists 'BEH-zee-ay', /ˈbɛz.i.eɪ/). Hugely common in front-end and graphics work — every CSS author meets cubic-bezier() and SVG path Béziers — yet it's routinely mispronounced 'BEEZ-ee-er' or 'BEZ-yer' with a hard, sounded 'r'. The accent on the é is also dropped in code (cubic-bezier), reinforcing the wrong reading. Stress is on the first syllable (BEZ); the alt form moves emphasis to the final 'AY'.

column7: Wikipedia — Voronoi diagram (named after Georgy Voronoy)
column10: Stress placement is genuinely contested. Named after Russian mathematician Georgy Voronoy (Воронóй). The most commonly documented mainstream English form puts primary stress on the FIRST syllable — VOR-uh-noy, /ˈvɔːrənɔɪ/ (so howtopronounce.com and the dev-community Casual Effects computational-graphics guide). But it competes with a final-stress vor-uh-NOY (ending like 'annoy') and a middle-stress vor-OH-noy — no single anglicization has won. The Russian-accurate rendering is closer to 'vah-rah-NOY', with the unstressed o's reduced toward 'uh/ah'. Pervasive in computational geometry, mesh generation, procedural texturing, and game dev, where 'how do you even say Voronoi?' (usually alongside Delaunay) is a recurring forum thread.

Hungarian mathematician Paul Erdős; Wikipedia gives the Hungarian IPA [ˈɛrdøːʃ ˈpaːl]. Two things break English speakers: the final 's' is /ʃ/ ('sh'), and 'ő' is a long front-rounded vowel /øː/ that has no English equivalent (nearest is the NURSE vowel in 'bird', or German ö). So it's roughly AIR-dursh — stress on the FIRST syllable — commonly anglicized to AIR-dish or UR-dosh, but never 'ER-doss' or 'ER-doze'. CS folks hit this constantly via the Erdős number and Erdős–Rényi random graphs (Rényi is already in the dict), and almost everyone says the spelling-based 'ER-doss' with a hard final 's'.

column7: Amplify Partners — "named for the Japanese word for 'sphere'"
column10: Paris-based open-science AI lab (founded Nov 2023 by Xavier Niel/Iliad, Rodolphe Saadé/CMA CGM, and Eric Schmidt/Schmidt Sciences) behind the Moshi real-time voice model. The name romanizes the Japanese 球体 (kyūtai), meaning 'sphere/orb' — the intended reading is two syllables: KYOO (きゅう, a long vowel like English 'cue'/'queue') + tye (たい, rhyming with 'tie'). English devs routinely mangle it into 3-4 syllables ('kee-YOO-tah-ee') because the vowel cluster looks longer than it sounds. Stress is genuinely contested: Japanese is pitch-accent rather than stressed, so English speakers split between first-syllable KYOO-tye (recommended primary) and second-syllable kyoo-TYE. Note kyutai.org itself does NOT explain the name; the Japanese-sphere etymology is confirmed by Amplify Partners' write-up.

Kyutai's real-time full-duplex speech-text foundation model (July 2024), built on the Mimi neural audio codec (Mimi = MEE-mee). Say it MOH-shee: stress the first syllable, long 'o' as in 'go', 'sh' as in 'ship' — MOH-shee. The name matches the Japanese word もしもし (moshi moshi), the phrase used to answer the phone; that etymology is widely assumed but is NOT stated in Kyutai's README, the arXiv paper (2410.00037), or kyutai.org, so treat the naming rationale as unconfirmed. Common English mispronunciations are 'MOSH-ee' (rhyming with 'posh') and 'MOSH-eye' — both wrong.

NVIDIA's 1.5B hybrid-head small language model (Nov 2024) that runs Mamba (state-space) and attention heads in parallel within the same layer. The name is widely read as a blend of 'HYbrid' + 'Mamba' (secondary write-ups confirm this; NVIDIA's own blog never states the etymology, so the portmanteau is community inference). That blend implies a short 'y' — HIM-buh, a homophone of the Namibian Himba people — and stress falls on the first syllable. But the 'Hy-' spelling leads many English speakers to default to HYME-buh (long i, like 'hybrid'), so usage is split. No official NVIDIA pronunciation exists.

Huawei's large-model family (盘古大模型, pinyin pángǔ), named for the primordial giant of Chinese creation myth. The two syllables are pan + gu (Mandarin IPA [pʰǎn.kù]), with the 'an' as in 'father'. The attested English pronunciation (Wiktionary) is /pɑːnˈɡuː/ — pahn-GOO — and the 'pan' has a plain /n/, NOT an 'ng'. English speakers very commonly misread 'pan-gu' as PANG-goo, fusing an /ŋ/ that isn't in the original and shifting the syllable boundary to pang|u. Distinct from the unrelated iOS jailbreak tool also called Pangu.

Lightweight CNCF Kubernetes container runtime; default runtime in OpenShift. The name derives from CRI (Container Runtime Interface) plus OCI (Open Container Initiative) — confirmed by Red Hat's announcement. Common community reading is 'CRY-oh': read 'CRI' as the word 'cry', then 'oh'. Stress on CRY. Common mispronunciations: spelling it out letter-by-letter 'C-R-I-O', or 'CREE-oh'. No official/maintainer doc states the pronunciation, so this is community usage rather than a creator clarification.

The language formerly known as Perl 6, renamed Raku in Oct 2019 with Larry Wall's blessing for the name. Community pronunciation is RAH-koo — first syllable 'rah' as in cheering 'rah-rah', second 'koo' as in 'cuckoo', stress on RAH. The name honors the Rakudo compiler, a contraction of Japanese Rakuda-dō ('Way of the Camel'), and evokes 楽 raku ('comfort/enjoyment'). English speakers frequently flatten it to RAK-oo (rhyming with 'rack you'), the common Anglicization. Do NOT read it as 'rake-oo'. Note: the cited Hackster.io article covers the rename and etymology but does not itself document the pronunciation.

column7: Wikipedia — Talk:Nostr (Pronunciation section documents the long-o vs ostrich split)
column10: Decentralized social protocol; the name is an acronym for 'Notes and Other Stuff Transmitted by Relays' — so it has no vowel and no official spoken form. Two camps: (1) NOH-ster with a long o like 'nose' (matching the 'Notes...' expansion), and (2) NOSS-ter with the short o of 'ostrich'. The ostrich reading is widespread enough that Nostr users nickname themselves 'nostriches', strong community evidence for the short-o variant. Wikipedia's Talk page logs both variants (nōstər, nôstər); creator fiatjaf has explicitly told people to 'invent your own way', so there is no creator decree and it is genuinely contested. Stress on the first syllable either way. (Other heard-in-the-wild forms include No-stir and Naw-ster.)

The 'Quite OK Image' format by Dominic Szablewski (phoboslab), released 2021 — a ~300-line lossless image codec. The widely-held reading is 'koy', identical to the ornamental koi fish: the official logo stylizes the Q into a fish, making the pun explicit (no formal written pronunciation note from the creator exists, so it's inferred from the logo and community usage). Rhymes with 'boy'. Despite this, many developers spell it out letter-by-letter as 'kew-oh-EYE' (Q-O-I) because it reads like a standard acronym. The sibling audio format QOA ('Quite OK Audio') follows the same koi-style joke.

column7: IETF RFC 8949 — Concise Binary Object Representation
column10: Concise Binary Object Representation, the binary data format defined in IETF RFC 8949 (used in COSE, WebAuthn attestation, CoAP/IoT). The RFC defines the expansion but gives no spoken form, so usage is split: many say it as one word 'SEE-bore' (C read as the letter, second syllable like 'bore' — roughly 'seaboard' minus the 'd'), while others spell it out 'C-B-O-R'. Compare the related token format CWT, which RFC 8392 explicitly says is pronounced like 'cot' — no such ruling exists for CBOR itself, leaving it genuinely contested. Stress on the first syllable in the word form (SEE-bore); on the final letter in the spelled-out form (see-bee-oh-AR).

column7: Google Open Source Blog — Introducing Brotli (2015)
column10: Google's HTTP compression algorithm (Content-Encoding: br), now supported in all major browsers. Named after the Swiss German word 'Brötli' meaning a small bread roll — sibling to Google's earlier 'Zopfli' (also a Swiss bakery product). Most English speakers say BROHT-lee (stress on BROHT, long o); some say BROT-lee with a short o. Two syllables: BROHT + lee — the 'tli' is one syllable, not 'tuh-lee'. The umlaut 'ö' of the original 'Brötli' is dropped in the ASCII spelling, which is part of what makes the word look harder than it sounds. Common mistakes: broh-TLEE (stress on the wrong syllable) or reading '-tli' as two syllables. Stress is on the first syllable.

column7: Hacker News — Kenton Varda (kentonv, creator) on the name origin
column10: Zero-copy data serialization + RPC framework by Kenton Varda, former maintainer of Google's Protocol Buffers. Per Varda himself, the name originally meant 'Capabilities and Protobufs' — NOT a Cap'n Crunch pun; he said the cereal reference 'never occurred to me,' and the 'cerealization protocol' pun was pointed out by someone else and only then added to the logo. Regardless of origin, 'Cap'n' is the ordinary contraction of 'captain', so it's said 'KAP-uhn PROH-toh' — same first word as the cereal mascot Cap'n Crunch — stress on KAP and PROH. Newcomers misread the apostrophe and say the full 'CAPtain Proto', or spell the tool name 'capnp' out as letters C-A-P-N-P. The schema file extension and compiler are 'capnp' ('cap-en-pee').

column7: Wikipedia Talk § Pronunciation (Peter Gustav Lejeune Dirichlet)
column10: Named after German mathematician Peter Gustav Lejeune Dirichlet — ubiquitous in ML via the Dirichlet distribution and Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA, Blei/Ng/Jordan 2003). The surname LOOKS French (from 'Richelette' in French-speaking Belgium) but he lived in German lands, so two camps exist: the German-style deer-ick-LAY (hard 'k', /kleɪ/) vs the French-style deer-ish-LAY (soft 'sh', /ʃleɪ/). Final stress on -LAY; the 't' is silent in both common readings (though a minority, citing Calabi, insist on a hard final 't'). Wikipedia's own talk page never resolved it — a contributor jokes that saying it 'correctly' makes you sound wrong to most mathematicians. Wikipedia documents the English IPA as the hard-k /ˌdɪərɪˈkleɪ/; many ML practitioners say deer-ish-LAY.

Named after French mathematician Évariste Galois (1811–1832), of Galois theory and the finite/Galois field GF(p) that underpins error-correcting codes, AES, and elliptic-curve crypto. English speakers regularly say gal-OYZ or GAL-oyss, treating it like an English word; the accepted reading is gal-WAH — the final 's' is SILENT (French) and stress lands on the second syllable -WAH. Wiktionary gives English /ɡælwɑ/; French is [ɡalwa]. Crypto/TLS engineers who say 'Galois Counter Mode (GCM)' very often mispronounce it.

column7: Wikipedia § Levenshtein distance (named after Vladimir Levenshtein)
column10: Named after Soviet/Russian mathematician Vladimir Levenshtein (Russian: Левенштейн), who defined the edit-distance metric in 1965 — bread-and-butter in NLP, spell-checkers, fuzzy search, and bioinformatics. The German/Yiddish-orthography '-stein' is the trap: the standard English reading rhymes 'stein' with 'mine' (like Einstein), giving LEV-en-shtine — stress on the FIRST syllable, 'sht' cluster kept intact. Many devs instead say LEV-en-STEEN / LEE-ven-steen (sounding the 's' separately, '-steen' like the recurring 'Bernstein' debate) or stress the middle syllable. The 'sh' before the 't' is what most often gets dropped.

Statistics term for the 'tailedness' of a distribution, from Greek κυρτός (kurtos, 'curved/arching'); coined by Karl Pearson c.1905. Wiktionary GenAm /kɚˈtoʊsɪs/ — primary stress on the second/middle syllable -TOH-, with a long 'oh'. Data scientists frequently flatten it to KUR-toh-sis (stress on first) or say ker-TAH-sis / ker-TOSS-is with a short 'o'. The leading 'ku-' is a plain 'ker-' schwa+r, not 'koor-'. Pairs with 'skewness' in every pandas .describe() conversation, so it's said often and missed often.

The Weibull distribution (reliability engineering, survival analysis, hazard/lifetime modeling, ML) is named after Swedish engineer Waloddi Weibull (1887–1979). General American speakers overwhelmingly say WHY-bull (/ˈwaɪbʊl/) with a true 'w' — this is the headword pronunciation in English Wikipedia, stress on the first syllable. But because Swedish 'W' is sounded as a 'V', the namesake-correct form is VY-bull (/ˈveɪbʊl/), which purists and many reliability engineers prefer; both are commonly heard in stats/ML talks. Either way the '-bull' is a reduced 'buul' (/-bʊl/), not 'bool'.

Tikhonov regularization (= ridge regression / L2 penalty; also ill-posed inverse problems) is named after Russian mathematician Andrey Tikhonov. Russian Ти́хонов = [ˈtʲixənəf] (per Wiktionary): stress on the FIRST syllable — TEE-khuh-nawf (the reduced final vowel is closer to 'nuf') — with a guttural 'kh' (/x/, a single throaty sound, not 'k-h') and a final 'v' that devoices toward 'f'. English speakers almost always shift stress to the second syllable, tik-HOH-nov, and say a plain 'k' + 'h'. The name also surfaces transliterated as 'Tychonoff' (Tychonoff's theorem in topology), reinforcing that the 'kh' is one guttural sound.
GenAm "MITH-ahs" (Merriam-Webster primary). Greek-derived term for a culture's underlying narrative/belief system. One reading pinned per request.

column7: Assran et al., 'Self-Supervised Learning from Images with a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture' (arXiv 2301.08243, CVPR 2023)
column10: Meta's image self-supervised model (Image JEPA); the 'I' is the letter 'eye' /aɪ/ and JEPA is one word /ˈdʒɛpə/ rhyming with 'pepper', so 'eye-JEP-uh' — the way LeCun voices the suffix. Predicts in latent space, not pixels.

column7: Meta AI blog, 'V-JEPA' (Bardes et al., arXiv 2404.08471)
column10: Meta's video JEPA; 'V' (Video) is spelled 'vee' and JEPA is said as the word, giving 'vee-JEP-uh'. Not letter-by-letter.

column7: Meta AI blog, 'Introducing the V-JEPA 2 world model' (arXiv 2506.09985)
column10: 2025 video world model, successor to V-JEPA; numeral read 'two' — 'vee-JEP-uh two'.

column7: Fei et al., 'A-JEPA: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture Can Listen' (arXiv 2311.15830)
column10: Audio/spectrogram JEPA built on I-JEPA; the 'A' (Audio) is the letter-name 'ay' /eɪ/, NOT the article 'uh' — 'ay-JEP-uh'.
Motion-and-Content JEPA; the initials have no vowel so they are letter-spelled 'em-see' ahead of the word JEPA — 'em-see-JEP-uh'.
JEPA for point clouds; ordinary word 'point' + JEPA — 'point-JEP-uh'.
Meta/FAIR self-supervised ViT foundation model, DINO scaled to v2; said 'DEE-noh vee-two' (DINO = self-DIstillation with NO labels). A minority says 'DYE-noh'.

Meta's 2025 ~7B-param self-supervised vision backbone, third in the DINO line; 'DEE-noh vee-three' is most common, but the dinosaur-pun reading 'DYE-noh' is genuinely widespread, so the vowel is unsettled.
ByteDance image-BERT pretraining with an online tokenizer (i = image, lowercase like iPhone); the repo's robot mascot signals 'EYE-bot'.
Microsoft masked-image-modeling pretraining (BERT for image Transformers, i = image); no official reading — split between 'beet' (most common, one syllable) and 'bait'.
NYU/Meta-FAIR latent world model built on frozen DINOv2 patch features for zero-shot planning; said 'DEE-noh W-M', often expanded to 'dino world model'.

column7: Grill et al., 'Bootstrap Your Own Latent', DeepMind (arXiv 2006.07733)
column10: Negative-pair-free SSL that bootstraps a target network from an online network (DeepMind 2020); the authors fixed no pronunciation, so it is both spelled out 'B-Y-O-L' and said as a word 'bee-OHL' — the follow-up BYOL-A audio paper even puns 'pronounced viola'.

column7: Chen & He, 'Exploring Simple Siamese Representation Learning', FAIR (arXiv 2011.10566)
column10: Negative-free Siamese SSL that avoids collapse via a stop-gradient; portmanteau of 'Simple Siamese', so the tail is read like 'Siam' — 'sim-SY-am'.
SSL that prevents collapse with separate Variance-Invariance-Covariance Regularization terms; said as the word 'VIK-reg' (hard g from 'regularization'), not spelled out.
VICReg extended to learn local/dense visual features for detection and segmentation; 'VIK-reg' followed by a spelled-out 'L' (for Local).

column7: Zbontar, Jing, Misra, LeCun & Deny, 'Barlow Twins' (arXiv 2103.03230)
column10: SSL whose loss drives the cross-correlation matrix of two identical network 'twins' toward the identity; plain English, named for neuroscientist Horace Barlow's redundancy-reduction principle — 'BAR-loh twinz'.

column7: He et al., 'Momentum Contrast for Unsupervised Visual Representation Learning' (arXiv 1911.05722)
column10: FAIR contrastive SSL with a momentum-updated encoder and a queue of negatives; a blend of MOmentum + COntrast, said as a word — 'MOH-koh'.

column7: Chen et al., 'A Simple Framework for Contrastive Learning of Visual Representations' (arXiv 2002.05709)
column10: Google/Hinton contrastive SSL framework; 'Sim' (Simple) + 'CLR' (Contrastive Learning of Representations) puns on 'clear', so 'sim-CLEAR' dominates; a minority spells it 'sim-C-L-R'.

column7: He et al., 'Masked Autoencoders Are Scalable Vision Learners' (arXiv 2111.06377)
column10: FAIR masked-image SSL (Masked AutoEncoder) that reconstructs pixels — the model JEPA argues against. Genuinely unsettled: many say the word 'may', others spell 'em-ay-EE'; no author statement, and it collides with 'mean absolute error'.

column7: Baevski et al., 'data2vec: A General Framework for Self-supervised Learning in Speech, Vision and Language', Meta AI (arXiv 2202.03555)
column10: Meta AI cross-modal SSL that predicts its own latent representations (closest non-JEPA cousin in spirit); inherits the word2vec/wav2vec lineage, so '2' is 'to' and 'vec' is 'vek' — 'DAY-tuh-too-vek'.

column7: Micheli, Alonso & Fleuret, 'Transformers are Sample-Efficient World Models' (arXiv 2209.00588)
column10: Sample-efficient Atari agent that learns inside a discrete-autoencoder + GPT-like Transformer world model; styled as the real word 'iris' (the official repo is lowercase) — 'EYE-ris', not lettered out.

column7: Robine, Höftmann, Uelwer & Harmeling, 'Transformer-based World Models Are Happy With 100k Interactions' (arXiv 2303.07109)
column10: A Transformer-XL-based world model for data-efficient Atari RL; the initials have no vowel to form a word, so they are spelled out letter by letter — 'T-W-M'.

column7: Google DeepMind — Genie 2 blog (foundation world model announcement)
column10: DeepMind's large-scale foundation world model that generates action-controllable, playable 3D environments from a single image; 'Genie' (from 'Generative Interactive Environments') is said like the common word genie, '2' as 'two' — 'JEE-nee two'.

Aleph is Runway's July-2025 in-context video model, named after aleph (א), the first letter of the Hebrew/Semitic alphabet. Stress falls on the first syllable: AH-lef, with a broad "ah" /ɑː/; the -lif variant (AH-lif) is equally attested. Non-native devs often mis-stress it as "uh-LEFF" (second-syllable stress with a reduced first vowel) the second-syllable stress is wrong, while a long-a "AY-lef" /ˈeɪlɛf/ is a valid but less common dictionary variant. Non-obvious because the spelled "a" is the open /ɑ/, not a schwa or the English long-a, and Runway's own announcement never states a pronunciation or name origin.

Aphrodite (aphrodite-engine) is PygmalionAI's large-scale LLM inference engine, built on vLLM's PagedAttention and named after the Greek goddess of love. Four syllables with primary stress on the third: af-ruh-DY-tee, ending in a full "-ee" sound. Non-native devs mangle it to "AY-pro-dite" — but "aph" is "af" (not "ayf"), and the final -e is NOT silent (it's "ee"). It's non-obvious because the "-ite" ending looks like it should rhyme with "kite," and English speakers instinctively silence a trailing -e. Verified against Wiktionary US IPA /æfɹəˈdaɪti/.

BAML is Boundary's (YC) open-source domain-specific language for writing type-safe LLM/agent prompts; .baml files declare typed prompt functions callable from any language. The README's own gloss is the joke "Basically A Made-up Language"; it is also widely reported to have originated as "Boundary AI Markup Language," paralleling YAML. Built on the -ML markup-language lineage, so the natural one-word reading is "bammel" /ˈbæməl/ — first-syllable stress, rhyming with camel, exactly like YAML→"yammel" / SAML→"sammel." No official pronunciation guide exists and many first-time readers instead spell it out B-A-M-L (alt), so it is genuinely split. Non-obvious because the four consonant-heavy letters give no cue that a vowel sits between the M and L; non-native devs also tend to over-articulate the trailing -L.

Coze (Chinese 扣子, kòuzi, "button") is ByteDance's no-code AI agent and chatbot-building platform, launched in 2024 (open-source Coze Studio at github.com/coze-dev/coze-studio; coze.com). It is ONE syllable, KOHZ — identical to the rare/literary English verb "coze" ("to chat cozily"), rhyming with "doze" and "rose"; the trailing "e" is silent and there is no second vowel. Non-native devs routinely misread the -oze/-e ending as a two-syllable "CO-zee" (like "cozy"), which is wrong. It is non-obvious because the spelling looks like it should split, and the Chinese source name kòuzi actually does have two syllables. Pronunciation confirmed against Merriam-Webster (/ˈkōz/) and Dictionary.com (/koʊz/, respelling [kohz], one syllable); no official ByteDance pronunciation statement was found, hence community-consensus rather than creator-clarified.

column7: NVIDIA Developer — cuDNN (CUDA Deep Neural Network library)
column10: cuDNN is NVIDIA's CUDA Deep Neural Network library, a GPU-accelerated set of primitives (convolution, attention, matmul, pooling, normalization) that underpins PyTorch, TensorFlow, and JAX. Say it COO-dee-en-en: the lowercase 'cu' inherits CUDA's 'coo' sound (as in barra-CUDA), then D-N-N is spelled out letter by letter, with primary stress on COO. Non-native devs commonly misread the prefix as 'see-you' (C-U) or 'cud', which sounds wrong to practitioners. It's non-obvious because 'cu' is a CUDA abbreviation glued onto DNN, not a standalone English syllable.

Dyad is a free, local, open-source AI app builder for power users (a v0 / Lovable / Bolt / Replit alternative), at dyad.sh (GitHub: dyad-sh/dyad). The name is the ordinary English/Greek word "dyad" (from Greek δυάς, "a pair/group of two"), stressed on the first syllable: DY-ad /ˈdaɪæd/, where "dy" is a long-i diphthong like "die." Confirmed by Merriam-Webster, Cambridge, Oxford, and Wiktionary. Non-native devs commonly misread the "dy-" as /diː/ and say "DEE-ad." Official site confirms the name but gives no pronunciation guidance; pronunciation is dictionary-backed.

Clip of "Einstein summation" — the tensor-contraction primitive in NumPy/PyTorch/JAX (np.einsum, torch.einsum); NumPy docs confirm it "Evaluates the Einstein summation convention on the operands" (docs state the meaning, not the pronunciation). Stress on the first syllable: INE-sum, where "ein" rhymes with the start of Einstein /aɪn/ (like "wine"). Non-native devs commonly say "EEN-sum" (/iːn/) or "AYN-sum" (/eɪn/, as in "rein"); the German "ei" digraph is /aɪ/, not /iː/ or /eɪ/. Non-obvious because the clipped "ein-" hides its origin in the surname Einstein.

column7: NVIDIA Research — Fugatto 1: Foundational Generative Audio Transformer Opus 1
column10: Fugatto = "Foundational Generative Audio Transformer Opus 1," NVIDIA's Nov-2024 any-to-any generative audio model (text/audio prompts to create or transform music, voice, and sound). Pronounced foo-GAH-toh, stress on the second syllable (GAH = long "ah" as in father), echoing the Italian musical term "fugato" (in the manner of a fugue) — NVIDIA doubled the t for the backronym (Italian speakers note the real word is one-t "fugato"). NVIDIA never published an explicit pronunciation, so this reading is inferred from the fugato etymon (community-consensus, not creator-clarified). Non-native devs commonly flatten it to FOO-gat-oh or few-GAT-oh, misplacing stress and using a short American "a."

column7: Wikipedia — GEMM (General matrix multiply, a BLAS subprogram)
column10: GEMM stands for GEneral Matrix Multiply, the BLAS Level-3 routine C = αAB + βC and the single most-invoked GPU kernel in deep learning ("fp8 gemm", "grouped gemm"). One syllable, said exactly like the jewel GEM — /dʒɛm/. The double M just comes from the BLAS function names (sgemm/dgemm/cgemm) and is NOT a geminated or hard sound. Non-native devs often mis-say it as spelled-out "G-E-M-M"; it looks like an initialism and the doubled M invites a wrong reading.

Horovod is Uber's open-source distributed deep-learning training framework (TensorFlow, Keras, PyTorch, MXNet) built on the ring-allreduce communication pattern. Uber's engineering blog confirms the name comes from a traditional Russian folk dance in which performers dance with linked arms in a circle — mirroring how the training processes pass gradients around a ring; the blog gives no pronunciation. First-syllable stress (HOR-uh-vahd) is corroborated by community pronunciation guides (e.g. HowToPronounce). Note the Russian original хоровод is actually final-stressed (kho-ro-VOD); English devs anglicize to first-syllable stress, and some mis-guess huh-ROH-vahd (second-syllable).

Hume AI is an empathic-voice research lab whose flagship product is EVI (Empathic Voice Interface, a speech-to-speech API through EVI 3 in 2025); it is named after the 18th-century Scottish philosopher David Hume and ships a "David Hume" EVI voice. One syllable, stressed HYOOM — a y-glide plus "oom", rhyming with "room" (/hjuːm/), with a silent trailing e. Non-native devs commonly mangle it into two syllables ("HYOO-mee", "HOO-may"); the single-syllable /hjuːm/ is non-obvious because the final e is silent and hu- is a y-glide, not "hoo".

Inngest is a durable-execution / workflow-orchestration platform for running reliable step functions and orchestrating AI workflows on serverless, servers, or the edge. The name is a stylized spelling of the verb "ingest" (it ingests events), so it is said in-JEST — /ɪnˈdʒɛst/ — with a soft g (dʒ) and stress on the SECOND syllable, identical to "ingest." The extra n is purely cosmetic and does not change the sound. Devs seeing the doubled "nn" often say INN-guest or force a hard g, which is what makes it non-obvious. The ingest pronunciation is a well-supported community inference from the obvious wordplay; no official creator statement on pronunciation was found.

Junie is JetBrains' AI coding agent (launched April 2025; moved out of beta to general availability June 17, 2026), running inside JetBrains IDEs (IntelliJ IDEA, PyCharm, WebStorm, GoLand, Rider, CLion, PhpStorm, RubyMine) and as a standalone CLI. Said "JOO-nee" — stress on the first syllable, long "oo" — exactly like the English given name/nickname Junie (as in the children's-book character Junie B. Jones): "June" + "-ee". JetBrains has not published an explicit pronunciation, so this reflects the standard name pronunciation (community consensus), corroborated by HowToPronounce (dʒˈuː.ni) and PronounceNames. Non-native devs are misled by the French-looking "-ie" tail into "joo-NYAY", or read the u with the "fun" vowel as "JUN-ee"; both are wrong. Non-obvious because the spelling doesn't cue the long-"oo" vowel or the front-stressed name.

column7: Kiro — official About page ("Kiro rhymes with hero")
column10: Kiro is Amazon/AWS's agentic IDE (2025), centered on spec-driven development: it turns prompts into structured requirements, design, and tasks that AI agents implement. Stress on the first syllable — KEER-oh (/ˈkɪroʊ/). The official About page explicitly says the name "rhymes with hero." Non-native devs are pulled by the spelling toward "KY-roh" (long-i, like gyro or Cairo), which is wrong; the written i maps to a near-/ee/ vowel, not the long-i diphthong.

Kokoro is the viral 82M-parameter, Apache-2.0 open-weight TTS model released in 2025 by hexgrad; the name is the Japanese word 心, meaning 'heart'/'spirit'. The even, flat-vowel KOH-koh-roh — matching the original Japanese 心 (ko-ko-ro) — is how practitioners actually say it, and is used here as the primary. The creator's model-card quickstart phonemizes it /kˈOkəɹO/ (Misaki notation, capital O = /oʊ/) = KOH-kuh-roh, with a reduced middle vowel — kept as the cited alternate. Non-native devs commonly stress the middle and add an 'or' vowel ('ko-KOR-oh'), which is wrong; stress stays light and front. Model card verified 2026-07: 82M params, Apache 2.0, name meaning, and the /kˈOkəɹO/ phonemization all present.

Le Chat is Mistral AI's consumer chatbot, a French pun since "chat" means both "chat" and "cat," which went viral in France in early 2025. Say it the French way, luh SHAH: the final t is silent and "ch" is /ʃ/ (like English "sh"). Wikipedia gives strict French /lə ʃa/; American speakers naturally render the vowel as "shah" /ʃɑ/. English devs commonly mangle it as "lee chat" or "luh CHAT" with a hard English "ch" and an audible t. Non-obvious because the spelling looks like the English word "chat," but it is French. (Mistral renamed the app Vibe at the end of May 2026.)

column7: Red Hat Developer — Introducing Machete (Neural Magic mixed-input GEMM kernel)
