column10: Neural Magic's mixed-input (w4a16/w8a16) GEMM kernel in vLLM 0.6.2+, optimized for NVIDIA Hopper GPUs; the spiritual successor to the Marlin kernels. Reuses the common English word 'machete' (the blade; a Spanish loanword). Standard General American puts stress on the second syllable — muh-SHET-ee — with 'ch' pronounced /ʃ/ ('sh') and the final -e as 'ee' /i/, not silent. Merriam-Webster and Wiktionary also list a 'ch' variant /məˈtʃɛti/ (muh-CHET-ee). Devs unfamiliar with the word commonly misread it as 'muh-CHEET' (rhyming with 'machine') or 'MATCH-et'. The source names and describes the kernel but gives no pronunciation guidance, so confidence is community-consensus (standard dictionary word), not creator-clarified.

column7: Fast Company — Moonvalley releases Marey ("pronounced Mary")
column10: Marey is Moonvalley's foundational AI video model (beta March 2025, GA July 2025), trained exclusively on licensed footage and marketed as the first "commercially safe" model for professional filmmaking; API available via fal.ai. Named after 19th-century French chronophotographer Étienne-Jules Marey. Fast Company and other press state it is pronounced exactly like the given name "Mary" — MAIR-ee, stress on the first syllable. The common mistake is a French-style "mah-RAY" (/maˈreɪ/) or spelling-based "MAR-ee," non-obvious because the honoree's French surname is [maʁɛ] (ma-REH) yet the product deliberately anglicizes to "Mary."

column7: TechTarget Storage Soup — founder A.B. Periasamy on the MinIO name
column10: MinIO is a high-performance, S3-compatible object-storage server; the name is short for "minimal I/O," so it is said min-EYE-oh — the "IO" is the spelled-out input/output acronym ("eye-oh"). Founder/CEO A.B. (Anand Babu) Periasamy has explicitly stated he wants it known as "Min-IO, as in minimal IO" (TechTarget, verified). The article confirms the common variant: "most people pronounce it MIN-EO" (min-ee-oh, captured as the alt) — devs default to it because "-io" reads like an ordinary word ending rather than a spelled-out acronym.

Orpheus is Canopy Labs' 2025 open-source, Llama-3b-based TTS model (repo canopyai/Orpheus-TTS; HF canopylabs/orpheus-3b-*), named after the legendary Greek bard-musician. Stress on the first syllable: OR-fee-us. Non-native devs often shift stress (or-FEE-us) or read 'ph' as /p/ ('OR-pee-us'); 'ph' is /f/ and the accent stays up front. Non-obvious because the classical name is unfamiliar and 'eu' invites a false /juː/ reading — a rarer two-syllable variant OR-fyoos /ˈɔːrfjuːs/ does exist, but OR-fee-us is dominant.

Phind was an AI search engine and coding assistant built for developers (YC S22, founded by Michael Royzen and Justin Wei; the product went inactive in early 2026). The name is a play on "find," with "Ph" taking the F sound as in "phone" — a single stressed syllable, FIND (/faɪnd/), a homophone of the word "find." Non-native devs sometimes misread the unusual "Ph"-before-consonant spelling as "P-hind" or split it into two syllables; it is simply pronounced identically to "find." Corroborated by a Latent Space podcast where founder Michael Royzen's spoken "Phind" is transcribed as "Find." The pronunciation is universally understood but not explicitly stated in any official source, hence community-consensus.

Qodo (formerly CodiumAI/Codium, rebranded 2024) is an AI code-review and test-generation agent platform. Its official rebrand blog confirms the name is a confluence of "Quality" and "Code" (and "quality of development") — but the blog does NOT state a pronunciation. Said KOH-doh: first syllable like "code," stress on syllable one. Pronunciation is community consensus (howtopronounce lists /ˈkoʊdoʊ/, "koh-doh"), consistent with the "Code" root — not an explicit creator statement. Non-native devs, thrown by a bare Q with no following U, often overcorrect to KWOH-doh (treating Q like "queen"/"quick"), but no /kw/ glide belongs here — this Q is a stylized C, not a normal English Q.

ROCm is AMD's open-source GPU-compute software stack — the CUDA equivalent — and the runtime behind AI training/inference on Instinct accelerators (e.g. MI300X). Said "ROCK-em" (/ˈrɑkəm/): stress on the first syllable, trailing lowercase 'm' voiced as a light "em" (never spelled out); "rock-M" is effectively the same sound. Common mistakes: reading it letter-by-letter (R-O-C-M) or giving "rock" a wrong vowel ("Rockham"/"Rockcam"). Non-obvious because the lowercase 'm' disguises the origin "Radeon Open Compute (platform)"; per Wikipedia the name no longer functions as a true acronym (Open Compute is a registered trademark).

Rodin (Hyper3D Rodin) is Deemos Technology's image-to-3D and text-to-3D generative model (2024-2025), named after French sculptor Auguste Rodin. Anglicized as roh-DAN: stress on the SECOND syllable, final '-DAN' rhyming with the name Dan (/dæn/), per Wikipedia's English IPA /roʊˈdæn/. Non-native devs wrongly read it as 'ROH-din' or 'ROD-in' with English spelling stress; it's non-obvious because the French-derived name stresses the final syllable and its nasal ending anglicizes to '-an' rather than '-in'.

SYCL is a Khronos Group royalty-free C++ standard for heterogeneous/accelerator (GPU) computing, central to Intel oneAPI and CUDA-to-SYCL porting. Pronounced SICK-ul (/ˈsɪkəl/) — a deliberate homophone of the farming tool "sickle" — with stress on the first syllable. Non-native devs commonly spell it out "S-Y-C-L" or say "SY-cull" (/ˈsaɪkəl/), missing that the two-syllable name reads like a real English word. Originally "System-wide Compute Language"; since 2020 the creators state it is a name, not an acronym. Pronunciation documented on Wikipedia; no creator/official pronunciation quote located, so labeled community-consensus.

DeepSpeed-Ulysses is Microsoft's sequence-parallelism system for training extreme-long-context Transformers — it partitions input sequences across GPUs and uses an all-to-all collective for distributed attention (arXiv:2309.14509). "Ulysses" is the Latin name for Homer's Odysseus. Primary stress is on the second syllable: yoo-LISS-eez. Non-native devs commonly mangle it as "oo-LISS-es," missing that the initial U is /juː/ ("yoo") and the -es ending is /iːz/ ("eez"). A first-syllable-stress variant (YOO-lih-seez) exists but the second-syllable reading dominates. Non-obvious because it is a classical proper name with irregular English vowels, yet cited constantly in long-context training papers.

Vapi (YC W21, vapi.ai) is a developer platform for building voice AI agents; co-founded by Jordan Dearsley (CEO) and Nikhil Gupta (CTO) — not a single founder. The name compresses "Voice API" (every feature is accessible via its API), confirmed by multiple sources. Stress falls on the FIRST syllable with a short /æ/: VAP-ee, rhyming with "happy" (/ˈvæpi/); corroborated by HowToPronounce (/vˈæ.pi/, "va-pee"). No official creator pronunciation statement was found, so confidence stays community-consensus. Non-native devs often default to "VAH-pee" (/ˈvɑpi/) or "VAY-pie" (/ˈveɪpaɪ/) because the bare letters V-A-P-I don't cue the short /æ/. For macOS say, respell as "vappy" for a reliable short-a.

column7: GDToday — Tsinghua/Shengshu unveil Vidu (name evokes "Video" and "We do")
column10: Vidu is the text-to-video AI model from Beijing Shengshu Technology and Tsinghua University (launched April 2024; Vidu Q1 shipped 2025). Stress falls on the FIRST syllable: VEE-doo. Shengshu chief scientist Zhu Jun explained at the 2024 Zhongguancun Forum that the name evokes "video" and the phrase "we do." Common mistake: non-native devs read it as VY-doo (long-i, as in "vie") or VID-oo (short-i, as in "video"); the correct branded vowel is a long /iː/, which is non-obvious because English "video" actually starts with a short-i /vɪd/.
